title: Matt King (rugby league)

[[Ethan King]] (nephew) }} }} '''Matt King''' (born 22 August 1980) is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] footballer. A [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] [[State of Origin series|State of Origin]] and [[Australia national rugby league team|Australian international]] representative {{Rlp|ce}} or {{rlp|wg}}, he played in the [[Super League]] for English club the [[Warrington Wolves]] and in the [[National Rugby League]] (NRL) for the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]] and [[Melbourne Storm]]. During his time in England he won the 2009 and 2010 [[Challenge Cup]]s with Warrington Wolves and was also named at {{rlp|ce}} in the 2010 [[Super League Dream Team]]. ==Background and early life== Matthew King was born in [[Casino, New South Wales]], Australia on 22 August 1980.{{citation needed|date=May 2023}} King began playing rugby league at the age of seven for his local club side the Casino RSM Cougars in northern [[New South Wales]].{{citation needed|date=May 2023}} When he was eleven he moved to [[Wauchope, New South Wales|Wauchope]] where he played Junior League for the Wauchope Blues in the under-11 and under-12 teams before moving back to Casino. He attended St Mary's Primary School and then went on to attend St John’s College Woodlawn for High School until Year 12 where he played in the First XIII Rugby League team under coach Kim Evans. In 2000, he was signed by the [[North Sydney Bears]] where he worked his way through the grades at the club playing in both Jersey Flegg and reserve grade. King suffered a serious shoulder injury in 2002 while playing off the bench for the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]]' reserve grade side and then quit rugby league because of a claimed disinterest.{{citation needed|date=May 2023}} After hanging up his boots at the age of 22, King took up several odd jobs such as working as a bar man and a garbage collector before deciding to give rugby league another go in 2003. He then signed with the [[Melbourne Storm]]'s feeder club, [[Brisbane Norths]] on the insistence of former North Sydney teammate [[Matt Orford]] who was now at the Storm. Ray Warren began to refer to him as "Ropehead", after his return to the [[NRL]] with dreadlocked hair.{{Cite news | last = AAP | author-link = Australian Associated Press | title = Blues rout Maroons | work = [[The Sydney Morning Herald]] | place = Australia | date = 6 July 2005 | url = http://www.smh.com.au/news/league/blues-rout-maroons/2005/07/06/1120329505015.html | access-date = 30 October 2010}} ==Professional playing career== ===Melbourne=== After moving to the Storm's feeder club in Brisbane, King played for nearly a year before being selected to make his début in the [[National Rugby League]] at the age of 23 against the [[St George Illawarra Dragons]] at [[WIN Stadium]] in round 24 of the [[2003 NRL season|2003 season]], impressing on his début without scoring. King went on to make another two appearances for the club in the regular season that year, scoring his first try in round 26 against the [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]]. [[File:Kingy.jpg|150px|thumb|left|King greeting fans after a Melbourne Storm game in 2007]] The [[2004 NRL season]] saw King develop further as a player at the Storm, he cemented his place in the squad and was vital in the Melbourne side's attack, becoming a potent offensive weapon with his agility, pace, strength and ability to find the try line. At the conclusion of the 2004 club season, King finished with a total of fifteen tries and had developed into one of the up-and-coming centres in rugby league. This was recognised with his selection in the annual [[City v Country Origin|City vs Country]] match for the Country side. He was also named as the Storm's rookie of the year.{{cite web|last=melbournestorm.com.au |title=NRL Honour Board |url=http://www.melbournestorm.com.au/default.aspx?s=nrl-honour-board |work=Club |publisher=[[Melbourne Storm]] |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130928161940/http://www.melbournestorm.com.au/default.aspx?s=nrl-honour-board |archive-date=28 September 2013 }} 2005 became the season where King would stamp his mark on rugby league, beginning the season by scoring seven tries in the first seven games of the season including a set of doubles against [[Brisbane Broncos|Brisbane]] and [[Canberra Raiders|Canberra]] and again impressing City v Country selectors enough to be again selected in the annual clash. After further impressive performances King was called up for his first major representative honour, being selected for the [[New South Wales]] [[Rugby League State of Origin|State of Origin]] side for all three games in 2005. In the decider he became the 9th player (and 4th New south Welshman) to score a hat-trick in state of Origin. King finished the 2005 season at Melbourne with fourteen tries, and coupled with his three for New South Wales, earned a spot in the Australian international side for the 2005 tour of Great Britain. He played in every game on tour and was again selected out of position on the wing, yet played well enough to earn praise from the Great Britain and Ireland international winger [[Brian Carney (rugby footballer)|Brian Carney]] who stated King was "the best winger in the world".{{cite news |format=fee required |publisher=AAP Sports News (Australia) |title=Carney rates King world's best winger |url=http://www.highbeam.com/doc/1P1-115280860.html |access-date=2007-12-09 |date=2005-11-15 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121023022043/http://www.highbeam.com/Search?searchTerm=RL+Carney+rates+King+world%27s+best+winger&searchType=Article¤tPage=0&orderBy= |archive-date=23 October 2012 |url-status=dead }} The following year in 2006 started off slowly for King, but as the year progressed he worked his way into form again. He was once again selected in the New South Wales side again for the annual State of Origin series, which NSW lost 2–1 to Queensland. After his side's 15–8 loss to the [[Brisbane Broncos]] in the [[2006 NRL grand final]], in which he scored a try, he cost his club $5,000 after publicly criticising match referee [[Paul Simpkins]].{{cite news|title='Dickhead' call costs King |url=http://www.smh.com.au/news/league/dickhead-call-costs-king/2006/10/04/1159641374957.html |work=[[The Sydney Morning Herald]] |date=2006-10-04 |access-date=2007-05-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121103215822/http://www.smh.com.au/news/league/dickhead-call-costs-king/2006/10/04/1159641374957.html |archive-date=3 November 2012 |url-status=live }} King was selected to play for the [[Australia national rugby league team|Australian national team]] on the wing in the 2007 [[ANZAC Test]] match against New Zealand, scoring a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in the Kangaroos' 30–6 victory.{{cite web|title=Anzac Test Match 2007|url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/matches/anzac-test-match-2007/australia-vs-new-zealand.html|publisher=Rugby League Project – Shawn Dollin, Andrew Ferguson and Bill Bates|access-date=1 September 2013}} After being consistently linked with a move to the [[Super League]] during the early half of 2007, Matt King finally announced on 10 July 2007, that he had signed a four-year deal with the [[Warrington Wolves]].{{cite news |url=http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,22048672-23214,00.html |title=King to join Warrington |publisher=Fox News |date=2007-07-10 |access-date=2007-07-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929111538/http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,22048672-23214,00.html |archive-date=29 September 2007 |url-status=live }} The deal was worth approximately A$600,000 a year, making King one of the highest paid players in rugby league. In September 2007, he finally realised his dream of playing in a premiership. Melbourne won convincingly over Manly-Warringah, 34–8, and King scored an inspiring try in his last game in the [[NRL]]. Overcome with emotions, King admitted when interviewed that he had been crying shortly after the victory and that this was "the best feeling ever".{{cite news |newspaper=Courier Mail |title=Veteran tips more to come |url=http://www.news.com.au/couriermail/story/0,23739,22510029-5013857,00.html |access-date=2007-12-09 |date=2007-09-30 |first=Steve |last=Ricketts |archive-url=https://archive.today/20121203031847/http://www.news.com.au/couriermail/story/0,23739,22510029-5013857,00.html |archive-date=3 December 2012 |url-status=live }} Melbourne was subsequently stripped of this premiership due to major and deliberate salary cap breaches.{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/storm/they-are-ours-why-cameron-smith-will-always-recognise-storms-stripped-premierships/news-story/6397becb6e5091d6804ef91c578c56bd/|title='They are ours': Why Cameron Smith will always recognise Storm's stripped premierships|work=Fox Sports|date=31 July 2019 }} ===Warrington Wolves=== [[File:Matt King Warrington.jpg|250px|thumb|right|King while playing for Warrington in 2008]]King started playing in England with the [[Warrington Wolves]] for 2008's [[Super League XIII]]. In the semi-final of the [[2009 Challenge Cup]] against [[Wigan Warriors]], King scored a [[hat-trick]] of tries to help Warrington Wolves book a place in the Wembley final for the first time in nearly 20 years.{{cite news|author=Mike Parsons|work=Warrington Guardian|title= Great to be a part of it, says Warrington Wolves' Matt King|url=http://www.warringtonguardian.co.uk/sport/wolves/wolvesnews/4538960.King__Great_to_be_a_part_of_it/|access-date=7 December 2013|date=11 August 2009}} He played in the Challenge cup when the Wolves defeated the Giants on 29 August, 25–16[http://www.warringtonwolves.org/newspage.ink?matchid=11616&type=m Warrington Wolves] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131212155230/http://www.warringtonwolves.org/newspage.ink?matchid=11616&type=m |date=12 December 2013}} ironically the same day his previous club, Storm, played their last game at [[Olympic Park Stadium (Melbourne)|Olympic Park Stadium]].[http://www.melbournestorm.com.au/default.aspx?s=article-display&id=19794 The Graveyard claims its final victim – Melbourne Storm] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110928040758/http://www.melbournestorm.com.au/default.aspx?s=article-display&id=19794 |date=28 September 2011}} On 28 November 2008, King married his longtime girlfriend Kirsten Deane in [[Richmond, Victoria|Richmond]] who he had met while living and playing in Melbourne. His bridal party included former teammates [[Ian Donnelly]], [[Dallas Johnson]] and [[Cooper Cronk]], while guests included [[Craig Bellamy (rugby league)|Craig Bellamy]], [[Billy Slater]] and [[Matt Geyer]].[http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/matt-king-weds-his-belle/story-e6freye0-1111118172498 Matt King weds his belle {{pipe}} The Daily Telegraph] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120404143101/http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/matt-king-weds-his-belle/story-e6freye0-1111118172498|date=4 April 2012}} He played in the [[2010 Challenge Cup Final]] victory over the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8938067.stm|title=Leeds 6–30 Warrington|publisher=BBC|date=28 August 2010|access-date=22 November 2019|first=Ged|last=Scott}}{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/aug/28/leeds-rhinos-warrington-wolves-challenge-cup-final |work=The Guardian|location=London |title=Chris Hicks hat-trick leads Warrington to Challenge Cup triumph over Leeds |first=Andy |last=Wilson |date=28 August 2010 |access-date=22 November 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100831105934/http://www.guardian.co.uk/sport/2010/aug/28/leeds-rhinos-warrington-wolves-challenge-cup-final |archive-date=31 August 2010 |url-status=live }} King was selected for the [[Exiles rugby league team|Exiles]] squad for the [[International Origin]] match against [[England national rugby league team|England]] at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]] on 10 June 2011.{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/13278410|title=Brian McClennan completes Exiles squad to face England fixture|work=BBC Sport|date=4 May 2011|archive-date=13 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160113141331/https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/13278410|url-status=live}} On 29 June 2011 King announced that he would leave the [[Warrington Wolves]] at the end of 2011's [[Super League XVI]] to join up with departing [[Wigan Warriors]] coach, [[Michael Maguire (rugby league)|Michael Maguire]] at [[Russell Crowe]]'s [[NRL]] team, the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]].[http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=22709 29 June 2011.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120910173656/http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=22709 |date=10 September 2012}} ===South Sydney=== [[File:Matt King.jpg|thumb|right|King playing for South Sydney]] King joined the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]] from 2012 onwards on a three-year contract at the NRL club, joining former Melbourne teammates [[Greg Inglis]] and [[Michael Crocker]] who had both been playing for the Redfern club for a few seasons. It was announced that he would captain the South Sydney Rabbitohs in the [[2012 NRL season]]{{cn|date=August 2024}} along with [[Michael Crocker]], [[John Sutton (rugby league)|John Sutton]], [[Roy Asotasi]] and [[Sam Burgess]]. An arm injury kept King out of most of the [[2013 South Sydney Rabbitohs season]]. King played 8 games for Souths feeder club side the [[North Sydney Bears]] in the 2013 [[NSW Cup]] scoring 5 tries.{{cite web|url=http://www.ssralmanac.com/mobile/pastplayers_bears.htm|title=South Sydney Rabbitohs Reserve Grade – North Sydney Bears Affiliated Players – 2007 to End of Season 2017|publisher=ssralmanac.com|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}{{cite news|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/matt-king-ruled-out-for-four-months-20130325-2gpgk.html?deviceType=text|title=Matt King ruled out for four months|newspaper=The Sydney Morning Herald|date=31 December 2013|access-date=1 January 2014}} Unable to regain the form he had at Melbourne, he retired at the end of the season. In 2015, King returned to the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]] side for the Auckland Nines Competition scoring four tries, including the grand final winning try against Cronulla-Sutherland in extra time.{{cite web|url=http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/farewell-to-the-fro-south-sydney-rabbitohs-back-matt-king-retires/story-fni3gki8-1226704394192|title=Farewell to the fro: South Sydney Rabbitohs back Matt King retires|work=The Daily Telegraph|location=Sydney|date=31 December 2013|access-date=1 January 2014}} == Statistics == {| class="wikitable" |+ !Year !Team !Games !Tries !Goals !Pts |- |2003 | rowspan="5" |{{leagueicon|Melbourne|size=16}} [[Melbourne Storm|Melbourne]] |4 |1 | |4 |- |2004 |20 |15 | |60 |- |2005 |23 |14 | |56 |- |2006 |21 |10 | |40 |- |2007 |23 |20 |1 |82 |- |2008 | rowspan="4" |{{Leagueicon|Warrington|12}} [[Warrington Wolves]] |28 |11 | |44 |- |2009 |26 |13 | |52 |- |2010 |31 |20 | |88 |- |2011 |21 |22 | |96 |- |2012 | rowspan="2" |{{leagueicon|South Sydney|size=16}} [[South Sydney Rabbitohs|South Sydney]] |20 |3 | |12 |- |2013 |3 | | | |- | |Totals |220 |129 |1 |518 |} ==Post playing & Coaching career== King joined the coaching staff of the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]] following his retirement at the end of the 2013 regular NRL season, contributing to the club winning the [[2014 NRL premiership]]. Following a South Sydney coaching restructure at the end of the 2016 season, King accepted an offer to join the [[Sydney Roosters]] coaching staff.{{Cite web |last=Proszenko |first=Adrian |date=2016-10-26 |title=Matt King shocks South Sydney Rabbitohs with switch to NRL rivals Sydney Roosters |url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/matt-king-shocks-south-sydney-rabbitohs-with-switch-to-nrl-rivals-sydney-roosters-20161026-gsb2l2.html |access-date=2025-01-06 |website=The Sydney Morning Herald |language=en}} In 2021, King was appointed assistant coach for Lebanon during the 2021 World Cup.{{Cite web |date=2021-05-21 |title=Matt King takes Lebanon coaching role for World Cup |url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/matt-king-takes-lebanon-coaching-role-for-world-cup |access-date=2025-01-06 |website=Love Rugby League |language=en}} In 2023, King was added to the NSW Blues high performance coaching staff when Michael Maguire became coach of the Blues.{{Cite web |date=2023-11-30 |title=NSW Blues confirm Maguire and Origin High Performance Team |url=https://www.nswrl.com.au/news/2023/11/30/nsw-blues-confirm-maguire-and-origin-high-performance-team/ |access-date=2025-01-06 |website=New South Wales Rugby League |language=en}} ==Quotes==
"For a winger to have the impact he did in a game was incredible, his input to the team's performance was significant, especially in the second game. He had such an important bearing on the result. I knew he was a good player but I didn't know he was that good." (New South Wales coach [[Ricky Stuart]], commenting on King's performances after the 2005 State of Origin){{cite news|author=Walter, Brad |newspaper=The Sydney Morning Herald |title=King for a night: giant winger punishes Maroons |url=http://www.smh.com.au/news/league/king-for-a-night-giant-winger-punishes-maroons/2005/07/07/1120329510848.html |access-date=2007-12-09 |date=2005-07-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121103215835/http://www.smh.com.au/news/league/king-for-a-night-giant-winger-punishes-maroons/2005/07/07/1120329510848.html |archive-date=3 November 2012 |url-status=live }}
"I haven't had the rise of a normal first-grader so I really appreciate where I am at the moment and it's something I don't take for granted."
